chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Hello. How's it going? How are you doing, Stan? Not bad. I appreciate you taking the time to connect.

STAN: Awesome. Where in the world are you? I'm not sure if it's me or your phone. Hello? There you are.

TABARAK: I see you twice. Yeah, no worries. Awesome.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Well, hey, Stan, thanks again for connecting. I appreciate you taking the time. I want to introduce myself. I'm Tabark Farah. I guess I can give you a little bit of history about me. My last previous adult job, I was a regional vice president over at DocuSign. Back in 2022, I got let go. It was honestly probably one of the best situations that could happen for me. I had golden handcuffs. I couldn't leave. I ended up leaving there, traveling the world. I'm actually based out of Bangkok, Thailand right now. I ended up traveling over here, and in that time, I decided... Hello? Oh, I'm sorry. I saw the other screen disappear. I ended up deciding, hey, you know what? I traveled the world a little bit. I saw Europe, saw Southeast Asia, and ended up deciding after some travel, I have to work. I can't just keep traveling forever. I called up a buddy of mine. We ended up talking through a couple of ideas for a business, got him to quit DocuSign. He came over here to Bangkok as well. In that interim time, when we're thinking about what business to build, we obviously started doing some consulting. That consulting work led to some fractional work. As we were trying to juggle clients and then grow our business, we just found this big disparity of trying to manage two things at once, grow the thing that was important for us, which is our business, and also the importance of taking care of our clients. What we found in that space was, were we biased or were other fractionals dealing with this? This led to the conversation I'm having with you right now. We've talked to about four dozen fractionals now. What I've been doing is, through all these conversations, I've been essentially collecting a lot of challenges that fractionals are dealing with, some of the solutions that they're dealing with. My hope was to have a conversation with you about some challenges you might be having, share some challenges I'm seeing from the fractional space, some solutions they're solving from the fractional space, and then also go over our approach of how we're thinking about doing this with our solution and our software that we're building. How does that sound?

STAN: That's fine.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: Awesome. What we've been doing is, we're trying to paint this problem space. Hopefully, by the end of this call, we can find out if you fit this problem space or not. If not, no big deal. If it is something that sounds like you're dealing with, the next thing we'd like to do is present the demonstration of the product for you. If that's something you're interested in, we're inviting about 10 to 12 fractionals to do our customer development program, where we give them our product for about 10 weeks, let them have at it, and then we build around them for our core customers. How about we just kick this off? Sam, why don't you tell me a little bit about yourself? I got some chance to peruse your LinkedIn, but I'd love to hear from you about your career, how you got into fractional work, and what you're working on now.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
STAN: I've been doing this for about 20 years.

TABARAK: 20 years?

STAN: I'm not in the same field as a majority of people. I have a whole background of seven people working for me. I have a complete infrastructure. Are you feeling a need? I think they are. I mean, the challenges of our faces, there's a lot of flab on us.

TABARAK: I'm sorry, I missed that last part. What did you say?

STAN: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

TABARAK: How many clients are you working with now? You said you have a whole infrastructure. You have people working for you as well? Okay, cool. You said 20 years you've been doing this. Is there a specialty that you work on specifically?

STAN: Not at all.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: How many clients do you usually manage, or what's your ideal amount of clients, typically?

STAN: Five, two days a week.

TABARAK: Yeah. Okay, that's pretty cool. And so your 10 clients, do you have a preference for how many project based versus embedded that you're in, or do you do all projects?

STAN: Yeah.

TABARAK: Wow. So is that what makes it easier for you to manage 10 of them at a time, is that you've had them for so long, you kind of understand that entire process?

STAN: Yeah.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Awesome, awesome. How do you get your clients? Is it mainly referral, or are you out there making the phone calls, doing the DMs on LinkedIn, posting?

STAN: Yeah. They help you with that part?

TABARAK: Wow. I know, I saw a few months ago, you were on a tear. I think you posted maybe, let me say like 12 posts?

STAN: No, no, no.

TABARAK: Yeah, 12 posts in a couple of months ago, about eight the last time, and then this month, you're doing some more. So they're all driving from your posts?

STAN: Yeah, everything's coming from our posts.

TABARAK: Yeah. I love that, I love that. So how long has it been, have you been using that process?

STAN: Yeah.

TABARAK: And is she doing more of the responding, and then reaching out, and like getting them on the phone? She's doing what?

STAN: That's awesome. I love that. Yeah, it's not, not yet, not yet. It's getting better every day.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: So let me ask you this, Stan, how do you manage your week? Do you plan all on Sunday? How do you just, if you have two clients?

STAN: Yeah.

TABARAK: Does your week ever change?

STAN: Yeah.

TABARAK: Yeah. Wow. So for you, what, so, okay, your biggest challenge you're saying right now is you're cleaning up a lot of mess when you're walking into these. And so you're burdened with a lot of-

STAN: Yeah.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Yeah. It's, it's a very small chance. I mean, for a 1% chance, a 10% chance, even then, you know, how many, so how much, Stan, how many hours a week are you working? You're saying you're working a lot.

STAN: I work in China.

TABARAK: Is that because of the volume of work? Is that because of the amount of work that you're doing? Your processes are just, are overwhelmed because you just have so much work or what's causing those?

STAN: That makes sense. So you're doing all, you're doing all, okay.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Yeah. How do you, how do you, do you just live off of your calendar? Do you use any methodologies, Agile, Scrum? How do you, how do you track your work?

STAN: Zaho.

TABARAK: Sorry. It's the admin work, right? They're just doing all the-

STAN: Yeah.

TABARAK: Yeah. It was phenomenal. Wow.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Yeah. So you're spending, what fraction of your time are you executing versus interfacing?

STAN: Yeah.

TABARAK: Yeah. Podcast.

STAN: Yeah.

TABARAK: And is that for to grow your business or just to add value, give value?

STAN: Yeah.

TABARAK: Yeah. Yeah. Stan, Stan, you work a lot. You work a lot. When you're doing the marketing, when you're doing the podcast, when you're doing your work, when you're doing the interfacing, I understand it. Obviously, I'm a founder, so I only have, I have to wear eight hats myself. So I understand the life you're living. Are you there?

STAN: Yeah, for sure. For sure. Yeah.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Yeah. I've heard that from many fractionals, many fractionals. I've heard that. And the one thing that I've heard from many fractionals is they don't have the capability that you have right now in getting clients.

STAN: Yeah.

TABARAK: That makes sense. Let me ask you a question. Let me ask you a question, Stan. If you had a magic wand right now and you could wave it and fix one thing that would unblock the thing that would, you know, 2x, 3x your business, what would that be?

STAN: To close, to close, close the deals.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: I have seen some ridiculous things happen before. But, uh, Stan I know. I've come past your time. I guess I'll kind of run through this real quick and just give you what we're building. But these are all the problems fractions are dealing with. You've obviously seen them, people not dealing with their time. Ultimately, what we're building right now, just to give you a heads up, is we're making a, I would call it like a fractional command center, right? It's essentially Google Maps for your work week. We take all your stuff from 10 different places, your Zoho, your Google, all the places come into one spot. So we have all the work in one area. And essentially what we do is we understand your goals, what goes against your time, what we call stakeholders. So that would be your client time, that would be your personal time, there'd be time with your employees, time for getting a posting in XYZ. Essentially what we do is we score all of that based on that hierarchy. So we know all of your tasks and prioritize it at the highest level, right?

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: Right now, I don't know if this is even a fit for your operations. I know you're very busy, but I don't know if it's prioritization or managing work from 18 different systems is your biggest thing, but this is kind of what we're working on right now. And then just as a heads up, this is our foundational level. Once we've secured this, now we're able to allow multiple things to happen. And our next thing after this is helping fractionals get clients. And the way we would do that is because we've built the system that allows to manage all your work, now there's no excuse of not doing your work plus getting new clients, right? At that point, we're helped with the ICP, we help with finding the right clients, we help with setting up like essentially a quota, right? How many do you need for the year? What do you wanna make? This is how many we should do each week or whatever. But that comes in a second phase.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: Right now, the core people we're trying to help out are people that have a lot of clients and a lot of things on their plate, and they're just not able to prioritize it at the highest level. They're working nights and weekends when it's not their choice. It's out of not having a system, essentially. That's what we're kind of helping out with our first round. I think that's all I have to say. Thank you very much. Thank you, Dan.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
STAN: Thank you. Awesome. I appreciate that. I'll find some time. Did I? Oh, I booked on your calendar. I'll find some time and I can book some time that fits on your calendar. And I'd be happy to demonstrate the product for you so you can take a look at it. And any feedback- Talk to us. Thanks, Dan. You have a good day. All right. Bye-bye.

TABARAK: Thank you for joining.
